title: A World Cup in Rinus Michels' image
gist: A three-item editor's notebook: Paul Lay argues the current World Cup's tactical homogeneity descends directly from Rinus Michels' 1970s Ajax/Netherlands 'Total Football,' transmitted via Johan Cruyff to Barcelona and Pep Guardiola's 'Tiki-taka'; he also marks historian Marc Bloch's reburial at the Paris Panthéon and recommends Dr John's 1973 album 'In the Right Place.'

insights.claim: The current World Cup's football is 'demographically diverse but strangely homogeneous in its tactics,' a legacy the author traces directly to Rinus Michels' Ajax teams of the late 1960s-early 1970s and his 1974 Netherlands World Cup side.
insights.speaker: Paul Lay
insights.so_what: Frames modern elite football's tactical convergence as traceable to a single coaching lineage, useful shorthand for why top leagues now look tactically similar.
insights.quote: demographically diverse but strangely homogeneous in its tactics
insights.locator: The World Cup, its drama accelerated

insights.claim: Johan Cruyff carried Michels' style to Barcelona, where it evolved into the 'Tiki-taka' later mastered by Pep Guardiola, which the author says now dominates Europe's major leagues and, by extension, the football world.
insights.speaker: Paul Lay
insights.so_what: Traces a specific transmission chain (Michels to Cruyff to Guardiola) for the dominant modern playing style, relevant to understanding coaching-tree influence in elite football.
insights.quote: His star player, Johan Cruyff, took the style to Barcelona, where it evolved into the 'Tiki-taka' mastered by Pep Guardiola, which now dominates Europe's major leagues

insights.claim: On 23 June, caskets symbolically representing historian Marc Bloch and his wife Simonne Vidal entered the Paris Panthéon, making Bloch the first professional historian so honoured, though their actual remains stay in the family vault at Le Bourg-d'Hem.
insights.speaker: Paul Lay
insights.so_what: Records a specific 2026 state-honours event and its symbolism for the historical profession.
insights.quote: two symbolic caskets entered the Paris Panthéon – representing the historian Marc Bloch, the first of his profession to be so recognised

insights.claim: Bloch, who founded the Annales school with Lucien Febvre, joined the French Resistance in Lyon in 1943 after being dismissed from his Sorbonne professorship by the Vichy regime, and was tortured and shot by the Gestapo in 1944.
insights.speaker: Paul Lay
insights.so_what: Documents the biographical stakes behind the Panthéon honour — a scholar executed for Resistance activity after being purged by a collaborationist regime.
insights.quote: he was tortured and shot by the Gestapo in 1944
insights.locator: Bloch had joined the Resistance in Lyon in 1943
